key_insight: Raising the argon flow rate from 1600 to 1800 L/h during air plasma spraying produces thicker, denser NiCrFeBSiC coatings with better friction performance.
engineering_relevance: Optimizing plasma-spray gas flow can improve coating quality and wear resistance without changing the coating chemistry, which is useful for protective surface engineering.
method: Air plasma spraying of self-fluxing NiCrFeBSiC coatings at two argon flow rates: 1600 and 1800 L/h
Structural and mechanical characterization including thickness, porosity, microhardness, and elastic modulus
Tribology and adhesion assessed by ball-on-disk friction testing and scratch testing
evidence_signal: At 1800 L/h, coating thickness increased by about 2×, porosity decreased by about 2×, and the friction coefficient dropped to approximately 0.107, while microhardness was 1015.8 ± 81.5 HV and elastic modulus was 203.9 ± 5.4 GPa.
watch_item: The study compares only two argon flow settings, so the optimal operating window and scalability to other spray conditions remain uncertain.
paper_title: EFFECT OF ARGON FLOW RATE DURING AIR PLASMA SPRAYING ON THE STRUCTURE AND PROPERTIES OF NICRFEBSIC COATINGS
